So, just be super careful.

author: @bearnaff9387
text:
As an aside, and not taking anything away from this accomplishment, HDPE is one of the commercially common thermoplastic that is probably most recyclable.  You can just melt and recast it indefinitely.  Whatever you make will end up taking on the color of the average of all the source plastics, but it apparently doesn't lose strength in 30 reheatings and recastings.  

That said, I would love to find out what aspect of the distilled HDPE the bacteria is using for feedstock, and whether other plastics can substitute.  Turning surplus casein, derived from milk, into a hard-wearing fiber via fermentation would be a major industrial boon to dairy-producing regions when the dairy market is saturated.

replies.author: @bearnaff9387
replies.text: @christopherd.winnan8701 Google says warping and poor bed adhesion.  I guess it's even more finicky than ABS?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: @bearnaff9387    HDPE has rather low surface energy.  Very little can bond to it.

replies.author: @bearnaff9387
replies.text:
@christopherd.winnan8701 It's worth noting that high-temp resins that can handle low-detail molding exist.   They are pricy when compared to resins, but super-duper cheap when compared to steel tooling.   As for desktop melters, injectors, mold clamps, and even shredders (to break down source plastics for melting) are all available either as purchasable tools or online plans.  

You can get set up for small run injection molding of HDPE for far less than the cost of a used car.

author: @armageddonready4071
text: When I got back from desert storm we were given gear to test out.  Some of the gear was polypropylene, and they said it was made from recycled two liter bottles.  Basically this stuff.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Polypropylene is the same as number 5 plastic (yogurt containers etc).  HDPE= #4 plastic.  Chemically and moeleculary, they are similar and have both some very similar and some very different properties.  Both are very light weight/low density, both are essentially chains of carbon and hydrogen, both are extremely hydrophobic.   But an interesting difference is their thermal conductivity.  Polypropylene has one of the lowest thermal conductivities of non exotic  solid materials while HDPE's thermal conductivity is about 4.7 x more and not far away from the thermal conductivity of water.  

   On in other words, PP based clothing is quite good for winter/cold weather and HDPE based clothing is good for summer/hot weather (unless it is so hot, that you actually want/need some thermal insulation to create a microclimate around your body/skin with your sweat evaporating and cooling things down).

author: @brianmorgan8950

author: @CapnSnackbeard
text:
Building the distillation process into a perma-industrial style recovered waste-heat system would essentially eliminate the fuel cost.

For example, Jean-pain system could be leveraged into heat, methane capture, high pressure cryogenic storage, phase change heat batteries, and TEG.

 Incorporating a rocket-mass furnace, waste gasses could be used for initiating secondary and tertiary combustion, architecture and design, and intelligent grouping of various industrial applications into a perma-systemic arrangement would capture and direct heating and cooling passively. Recapturing and reusing waste heat and waste material as resources, and using their output as a set of suggestions for a new process that might use it as an input.

In this way, it becomes a chain reaction of abundance in which giving nets far more than taking

author: @CCoburn3
text: I used to work for a company that was going to convert organic feedstocks into diesel. Using simple technology, you can convert basically any carbon-rich feedstock into fuel. But this seems to go a step further. Someday, we may not throw anything away -- it depends more on the economics than anything. We have to have a high-value end product to make it worthwhile to recycle. Perhaps this spider silk has a high enough value. It certainly is worth looking into.

replies.text: I randomly sat down with the owner of a burger joint who was about to sell off to focus on his day job - a company in North Texas that was creating biodiesel from agriwaste.  The innovation was in where they got the energy for the conversion - they were building a small solar thermal tower at their refinery.

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text: @bearnaff9387 I hope it works out for him. It's old technology -- developed in Germany during WWI. The use of solar power may save enough money to make it worthwhile though. It's certainly worth a try.
